title: Quiz: Rate-Distortion Theory
description: Test your understanding of lossy compression and rate-distortion theory

questions.question: For a binary symmetric source with p = 0.5 and Hamming distortion, R(0) equals:
questions.explanation: R(0) = H(p) - H(0) = H(0.5) - 0 = 1 bit. Zero distortion requires lossless compression, which needs H(X) bits.

questions.question: For a Gaussian source with variance σ², how much rate reduction is gained by doubling the allowed distortion?
questions.explanation: R(D) = 0.5 log₂(σ²/D). Doubling D: R(2D) = 0.5 log₂(σ²/(2D)) = 0.5 log₂(σ²/D) - 0.5. So we save 0.5 bits.

questions.question: The rate-distortion function R(D) is:
questions.explanation: R(D) is convex and non-increasing (decreasing). Allowing more distortion never requires more bits, and the tradeoff has diminishing returns.

questions.question: For Hamming distortion, R(D) can be negative for sufficiently large D.
questions.explanation: False. R(D) ≥ 0 always. When D is large enough (D ≥ min(p, 1-p) for binary), R(D) = 0 but never negative. Zero rate means the decoder can guess without any information.

questions.question: In JPEG compression, increasing the 'quality' parameter typically:

options.text: Keeps rate constant while decreasing distortion
Has no effect on the rate-distortion tradeoff

questions.explanation: Higher quality means finer quantization, which preserves more detail (lower distortion) but requires more bits (higher rate). This moves up the R(D) curve.

questions.question: The rate-distortion problem minimizes _____ subject to a distortion constraint.
questions.explanation: R(D) = min I(X; X̂) over all p(x̂|x) such that E[d(X, X̂)] ≤ D. Mutual information represents the rate needed to describe the source.

questions.question: For a Gaussian source, each additional bit of rate improves the SNR by approximately 6 dB.
questions.explanation: True. R = 0.5 log₂(σ²/D), so σ²/D = 2^(2R). Each additional bit doubles 2^(2R), which is 10 log₁₀(2) ≈ 3 dB per bit × 2 = 6 dB per bit.

questions.question: Which are valid distortion measures? (Select all)

options.text: Hamming distance: d(x, x̂) = 1 if x ≠ x̂

questions.explanation: Valid distortion measures must be non-negative. a, b, d are all non-negative. c) is related to log-loss but not a standard distortion measure in classical rate-distortion theory.
